passages.explanation: On the day of Pentecost — a major Jewish harvest festival — the believers are all gathered together. Suddenly a sound like a violent wind fills the house, and what looks like tongues of fire rest on each person. They're all filled with the Holy Spirit and begin speaking in other languages. This is the moment Jesus promised. The wind and fire imagery echoes Old Testament appearances of God.

Jerusalem is packed with devout Jews from every nation for the festival. They hear the commotion and are bewildered because each person hears the believers speaking in their own native language. Luke lists a remarkable range of nationalities — Parthians, Medes, Elamites, people from across the Roman world and beyond. Some are amazed and ask what this means. Others dismiss it, saying they're just drunk. It's about 9 in the morning.

Peter stands up and addresses the crowd directly. He dismisses the drunkenness accusation — it's only nine in the morning. Instead, he says this is what the prophet Joel predicted: in the last days, God would pour out his Spirit on all people. Sons and daughters will prophesy, young men will see visions, old men will dream dreams. Even servants will receive the Spirit. Peter is making a massive claim — the age of the Spirit has arrived.

Peter pivots to Jesus. He reminds them that Jesus of Nazareth performed miracles among them — they saw it themselves. He was handed over and killed according to God's predetermined plan, but they used lawless men (the Romans) to crucify him. Then the core message: God raised him from the dead, because death couldn't hold him. Peter quotes David from Psalm 16 about God not letting his Holy One see decay.

Peter argues that David wasn't talking about himself — David died and his tomb is still there in Jerusalem. David was a prophet who knew God had promised to put one of his descendants on his throne. David was speaking about the Messiah's resurrection. Jesus was raised, they're all witnesses to it, and now exalted to God's right hand, he has poured out the Spirit — which is what they're seeing and hearing. Peter's conclusion is devastating: God has made this Jesus, whom you crucified, both Lord and Messiah.

The crowd is cut to the heart and asks what they should do. Peter tells them to repent and be baptised in the name of Jesus for the forgiveness of sins, and they'll receive the Holy Spirit. The promise is for them, their children, and everyone far off — anyone God calls. About 3,000 people are baptised that day. The church explodes from 120 to over 3,000 in a single sermon.

Luke describes the new community's life: they devote themselves to the apostles' teaching, fellowship, breaking bread, and prayer. A sense of awe pervades everything, with many signs and wonders. They share possessions, selling property to give to anyone in need. They meet daily in the temple and in homes, eating together with glad and sincere hearts. They have the goodwill of the people, and the Lord adds to their number daily. This is Luke's first 'summary snapshot' of the early church — an idealised but genuine picture of what the Spirit-filled community looked like.
